startScreenOptions.title: BI5.1-BI5.9 — Interactive Lecture (BI)
startScreenOptions.shortStartDescription: Interactive lecture with 6 embedded questions for BI5.1-BI5.9.

interactions.label: In phenylketonuria (PKU), dietary phenylalanine restriction is combined with tyr

params.question: In phenylketonuria (PKU), dietary phenylalanine restriction is combined with tyrosine supplementation. Why is tyrosine supplementation necessary in these patients?

answers.text: Tyrosine is one of the nine essential amino acids and cannot be synthesised by the body

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Tyrosine is normally a non-essential amino acid — the body synthesises it from phenylalanine via phenylalanine hydroxylase. It becomes conditionally essential only when this enzyme is deficient.

answers.text: The blocked conversion of phenylalanine to tyrosine makes tyrosine a conditionally essential amino acid

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Phenylalanine hydroxylase converts phenylalanine to tyrosine. When this enzyme is deficient in PKU, the body loses its only endogenous pathway for tyrosine synthesis, making dietary supplementation essential.

answers.text: Tyrosine supplementation accelerates the breakdown of accumulated phenylalanine

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Tyrosine does not promote phenylalanine degradation. The purpose of supplementation is to supply what the body can no longer produce, since the Phe → Tyr conversion is blocked.

answers.text: Excess phenylalanine competitively inhibits intestinal absorption of dietary tyrosine

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: While amino acids can compete at intestinal transporters, the primary reason for supplementation in PKU is that endogenous tyrosine synthesis from phenylalanine is completely blocked.

overallFeedback.feedback: In PKU, phenylalanine hydroxylase is deficient. Since the body normally derives tyrosine from phenylalanine via this enzyme, the loss of this pathway makes tyrosine conditionally essential and supplementation necessary.
Well done! In PKU, phenylalanine hydroxylase deficiency blocks the Phe → Tyr conversion, making tyrosine conditionally essential.

interactions.label: Vitamin C is essential for normal collagen synthesis. What specific biochemical

params.question: Vitamin C is essential for normal collagen synthesis. What specific biochemical role does it serve in this process?

answers.text: It provides the glycine residues that occupy every third position in the collagen chain

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The Gly-X-Y repeat pattern in collagen is determined by the amino acid sequence encoded in DNA. Vitamin C does not supply amino acid residues.

answers.text: It hydroxylates proline and lysine residues, stabilising the collagen triple helix

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Vitamin C is the cofactor for prolyl hydroxylase and lysyl hydroxylase. These enzymes produce hydroxyproline and hydroxylysine, which form hydrogen bonds essential for stabilising the collagen triple helix.

answers.text: It crosslinks collagen chains through disulfide bond formation between cysteine residues

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Disulfide bonds form between cysteine residues and stabilise other proteins. Collagen stability depends on hydroxylation of proline and lysine — the reaction requiring vitamin C as a cofactor.

answers.text: It inserts the central iron atom into the collagen molecule to confer structural rigidity

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Iron insertion is a step in haem synthesis (catalysed by ferrochelatase), not collagen synthesis. Collagen does not contain a metal ion. Vitamin C's role is hydroxylation of proline and lysine.

overallFeedback.feedback: Vitamin C is the cofactor for prolyl and lysyl hydroxylases in collagen synthesis. Deficiency leads to weak collagen and the clinical features of scurvy: bleeding gums, loose teeth, and poor wound healing.
Correct! Without vitamin C, hydroxylation of proline and lysine fails, producing structurally weak collagen — the basis of scurvy.

interactions.label: Low serum albumin in a patient hospitalised with acute pneumonia reliably indica

params.question: Low serum albumin in a patient hospitalised with acute pneumonia reliably indicates protein-energy malnutrition.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: Not quite. Albumin is a negative acute-phase reactant: it falls during inflammation and acute illness, not necessarily due to malnutrition. The video explicitly states that low albumin in a sick patient does not always mean malnutrition.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct. Albumin is a negative acute-phase reactant — its levels fall during acute inflammation and illness regardless of nutritional status. C-reactive protein rises rapidly while albumin drops, making albumin unreliable as a nutritional marker during acute illness.

interactions.label: Each molecule of urea produced by the urea cycle carries two nitrogen atoms. Wha

params.question: Each molecule of urea produced by the urea cycle carries two nitrogen atoms. What are the sources of these two nitrogen atoms?

answers.text: Both nitrogen atoms are derived from free ammonia

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Only one nitrogen atom comes from free ammonia, incorporated into carbamoyl phosphate by carbamoyl phosphate synthetase I. The second nitrogen enters the cycle from a different source.

answers.text: One nitrogen comes from free ammonia and the other from aspartate

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. The first nitrogen enters as free ammonia via carbamoyl phosphate. The second nitrogen enters as aspartate, which condenses with citrulline to form argininosuccinate. Both pathways converge to produce the two nitrogens of each urea molecule.

answers.text: Both nitrogen atoms are derived from glutamine

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Glutamine is a transport form of ammonia in the blood, carrying nitrogen from peripheral tissues to the liver. Within the urea cycle itself, the two nitrogens come from free ammonia and aspartate.

answers.text: One nitrogen comes from alanine and the other from glutamate

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Alanine carries nitrogen to the liver via the glucose-alanine cycle, and glutamate releases ammonia through deamination. However, within the urea cycle, the two nitrogens are derived directly from free ammonia and aspartate.

overallFeedback.feedback: In the urea cycle, one nitrogen comes from free ammonia (via carbamoyl phosphate) and the other from aspartate (via argininosuccinate). Glutamine and alanine serve as blood transport carriers that deliver nitrogen to the liver.
Exactly right! Understanding the two nitrogen sources — free ammonia and aspartate — is key to understanding how the urea cycle integrates with transamination and deamination.

interactions.label: A newborn's urine has a characteristic sweet, maple-syrup odour. Which group of

params.question: A newborn's urine has a characteristic sweet, maple-syrup odour. Which group of amino acids accumulates due to the underlying enzyme deficiency?

answers.text: Aromatic amino acids such as phenylalanine and tryptophan

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Phenylalanine accumulates in phenylketonuria (producing musty-smelling urine), and tryptophan malabsorption occurs in Hartnup disease. The sweet, maple-syrup odour is specific to a different group of amino acids.

answers.text: Sulphur-containing amino acids such as methionine and cysteine

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Methionine and homocysteine accumulate in homocystinuria, which presents with downward lens subluxation and thromboembolism — not with sweet-smelling urine.

answers.text: Branched-chain amino acids such as leucine, isoleucine, and valine

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Maple syrup urine disease results from deficiency of branched-chain alpha-keto acid dehydrogenase. The accumulated branched-chain amino acids and their keto acid derivatives produce the characteristic sweet odour.

answers.text: Basic amino acids such as lysine and arginine

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Defects in basic amino acid transport (such as cystinuria) cause kidney stones, not sweet-smelling urine. The maple-syrup odour specifically indicates branched-chain amino acid accumulation.

overallFeedback.feedback: Maple syrup urine disease results from deficiency of branched-chain alpha-keto acid dehydrogenase. Leucine, isoleucine, and valine accumulate, producing the characteristic sweet odour. Early detection by newborn screening prevents irreversible neurological damage.
Well done! MSUD is caused by branched-chain alpha-keto acid dehydrogenase deficiency and is detected by heel-prick newborn screening at 48–72 hours.

interactions.label: A patient with carbon monoxide poisoning appears cherry-red rather than cyanotic

params.question: A patient with carbon monoxide poisoning appears cherry-red rather than cyanotic. Why does carbon monoxide cause tissue hypoxia despite this appearance?

answers.text: Carbon monoxide destroys red blood cells, reducing the total oxygen-carrying capacity

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Carbon monoxide does not cause haemolysis. It binds to haemoglobin at the same site as oxygen, forming carboxyhaemoglobin, which gives the cherry-red colour but cannot transport oxygen.

answers.text: Carbon monoxide binds haemoglobin with approximately 250 times greater affinity than oxygen, displacing it from binding sites

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Carbon monoxide's vastly greater affinity for haemoglobin means that even small amounts saturate binding sites, leaving insufficient capacity for oxygen transport. Treatment is 100% oxygen to competitively displace CO.

answers.text: Carbon monoxide oxidises haemoglobin iron to the ferric (Fe³⁺) state, forming methaemoglobin

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Oxidation of haem iron to Fe³⁺ produces methaemoglobin — a different condition that presents with chocolate-brown cyanosis and is treated with intravenous methylene blue, not oxygen therapy.

answers.text: Carbon monoxide prevents the synthesis of new haemoglobin in the bone marrow

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Carbon monoxide does not inhibit haemoglobin synthesis. Its toxicity results from binding to existing haemoglobin with 250 times greater affinity than oxygen, preventing oxygen transport at the molecular level.

overallFeedback.feedback: Carbon monoxide binds haemoglobin with approximately 250 times greater affinity than oxygen, forming carboxyhaemoglobin. Despite the cherry-red appearance, oxygen cannot bind, causing severe tissue hypoxia. Treatment is 100% oxygen.
Correct! CO binds haemoglobin with ~250× the affinity of oxygen. Despite the cherry-red appearance of carboxyhaemoglobin, tissues are starved of oxygen. Treatment is 100% oxygen.
